nodes.name: AcopioVzla
nodes.type: Frontend SPA (React/Vite) backed by Supabase BaaS
nodes.summary: Acopio-VZLA (also named "saman" internally) is a fully functional React SPA for registering and browsing Venezuelan earthquake aid-collection centers (centros de acopio). The app is already live on Vercel. It uses Supab…
nodes.onePitch: AcopioVzla conecta donantes con centros de acopio en tiempo real — porque en un terremoto, la información salva vidas.

database: PostGIS (geom column + GIST index for KNN)
stack.orm: Supabase JS SDK (@supabase/supabase-js) calling PostgREST and RPCs directly — no ORM. SQL logic lives in versioned PLpgSQL migrations and RPC functions (e.g. centros_cercanos using PostGIS KNN). RLS is the sole authorization layer.
stack.pattern: Feature-sliced SPA with BaaS (no custom server)

redFlags: No CI/CD pipeline — no automated test runs on push, no build verification before deploy
Committed coverage/coverage-summary.json with developer's local Linux path reveals rushed workflow
Single contributor — bus-factor of 1 with no review process
No error monitoring or logging (no Sentry, no structured logs)
Supabase free tier has row/storage/bandwidth limits that could be hit in a real emergency surge
No rate-limiting on write mutations (centro creation, post creation) beyond Supabase defaults — potential for spam/abuse in a public emergency context
nodes.mergePotential: Low — this is a self-contained SPA with its own Supabase backend. It could be featured as a standalone tool or linked from a portal, but its architecture (client-only, Supabase BaaS) makes it difficult to merge with server-rendered or differently-architected projects without significant rework.

diffusion.angle: Frame it as infrastructure solidaria: a free, open, real-time map connecting donors with the aid centers that actually need them — because in an earthquake, WhatsApp chaos costs lives. Highlight that any centro can register in minutes, needs update in real time, and anyone with a phone can find the nearest drop-off point.
gaps: No demo video — a 60-second screen recording would dramatically increase share rate
No screenshots for social posts — static images of the feed/map view are missing
No branding assets (logo, OG image) visible — social previews will be blank
No real-user adoption data yet — zero evidence of centros actually registered, which weakens social proof

nodes.name: AidTrace | RastroAyuda
nodes.type: Offline-first PWA + serverless API + Celo smart contract (blockchain-backed aid traceability)
nodes.summary: AidTrace / RastroAyuda is an offline-first humanitarian supply-tracking PWA built on Celo Mainnet. Coordinators create QR-labeled aid batches; field workers record custody handoffs (pickup, delivery, damage) via browser…
nodes.onePitch: AidTrace rastrea cada entrega humanitaria en blockchain — funciona sin internet, sin wallet, solo con Telegram — para que ninguna caja de ayuda se pierda sin prueba.

backend: Express v5 (listed as dependency but API routes are plain functions)
database: No traditional DB — Celo blockchain events are source of truth
stack.orm: No ORM. Blockchain events (viem getLogs / writeContract) are the persistence layer. Supabase and Redis are used exclusively for distributed locking of the single relayer wallet to avoid nonce collisions on Celo.
stack.pattern: Offline-first PWA + serverless relay + blockchain event ledger

redFlags: No authentication or rate limiting on /api/zavu relay endpoint — any caller can submit events at the relayer's expense
Timeline block scan fetches all historical logs on every API call — will grow unbounded and slow as usage accumulates
Single relayer private key with no rotation strategy; compromise requires redeploying with a new submitter key
No tests of any kind (unit, integration, or contract tests)
No CI pipeline
CORS wildcard on relay endpoint
manifest.webmanifest has empty icons array — PWA installability is incomplete
nodes.mergePotential: Medium-high. The blockchain traceability layer is a unique contribution that complements shelter-mapping or people-finder projects. The Telegram relay and offline-first queue pattern could be re-used as a messaging primitive by other projects in the hackathon.

diffusion.angle: Frame around the zero-barrier field worker experience: no wallet, no crypto knowledge, no internet required — just Telegram or a browser. Every box of medicine, every water delivery, provably recorded on blockchain. Lead with the Telegram command demo ('CELO1 entregar 13 cajas de ibuprofeno') as the human hook, then show the Celoscan proof link as the accountability payoff. Bilingual angle reinforces real-world deployment readiness.
gaps: screenshots of the QR label and Celoscan proof view to make social posts visual
branding assets (logo, OG image — PWA manifest has empty icons)
evidence of real users or pilot deployment
a short explainer GIF or screen recording clip for X/Twitter

nodes.name: Apoyo Venezuela
nodes.type: Web SPA — crowdsourced directory of donation-collection and volunteering points for Venezuelan diaspora
nodes.summary: ApoyoVenezuela is a live, community-run SPA (React 19 + Supabase) that maps donation-collection and volunteering points for the Venezuelan diaspora abroad. It is already deployed at apoyovenezuela.org. The codebase is c…
nodes.onePitch: Directorio vivo y verificado por la comunidad para encontrar centros de acopio y voluntariado de confianza en la respuesta al terremoto de Venezuela — sin admins, sin listas desactualizadas.

stack.orm: Supabase JS client (@supabase/supabase-js v2) for reads via PostgREST; Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) with raw .insert()/.select() calls for writes — no ORM, no raw SQL in the frontend
stack.pattern: BaaS-backed SPA with Edge Function write layer

redFlags: Rate limiting relies solely on SHA-256(IP+UserAgent) fingerprint — a motivated bad actor can bypass by rotating IPs or spoofing User-Agent headers; no hard server-level IP blocking or CAPTCHA
No admin/moderation UI — the 3-claim auto-hide mechanism is the only safeguard against spam; malicious actors could flood 3 claims per place to suppress legitimate aid points
Client-side filtering on entire dataset with no pagination — will degrade noticeably past a few hundred places
active_places view uses a correlated subquery with GROUP BY/HAVING on place_claims without a dedicated index, which will be slow at scale
No structured error logging or alerting — failures are silently swallowed beyond the user-facing error string
nodes.mergePotential: The project is self-contained and already live. It could serve as the reference implementation for a diaspora-aid-point directory module; its Edge Function + rate-limit + RLS pattern is directly reusable in any Supabase-backed humanitarian app. Low merge friction since it has no conflicting auth system and exposes a simple REST-like Edge Function API.

diffusion.angle: Community-powered trust: no one needs permission to add, confirm, or remove a donation point — the people who show up are the ones who keep the list honest. Lead with the self-correcting moderation angle (3 reports = auto-hidden) as it differentiates from static Google Sheets lists that flood every disaster response.
gaps: No screenshots or GIF preview for social posts (SPA requires JS to render, making automated screenshots harder)
Zero GitHub stars signals low external visibility so far
No visible user/confirmation count to cite as social proof
No map/geo view shown — a visual map would be a stronger promotional asset than a text list

nodes.name: Apoyo Venezuela
nodes.type: Mobile-first PWA — Next.js App Router + Supabase BaaS, disaster-relief coordination platform
nodes.summary: Apoyo Venezuela is a genuinely well-built Next.js 16 / Supabase PWA for citizen-coordinated earthquake relief, already deployed at apoyovenezuela.com. It maps affected zones with structural status, tracks aid needs per…
nodes.onePitch: Apoyo Venezuela turns scattered earthquake reports into one live map so rescuers and volunteers know exactly where help is needed — and what is already covered.

stack.orm: Direct Supabase JS client (BaaS). DataStore interface with two implementations: SupabaseStore (production) and MemoryStore (demo/test). Row mapping is manual snake_case→camelCase. No ORM.
stack.pattern: Next.js App Router — server components + server actions + API route handlers, clean store-abstraction layer

redFlags: listLocationsPage fetches all rows from Supabase then paginates in-memory — will degrade significantly under load as the dataset grows
AI rate limiter is per-serverless-instance only; global rate ceiling must be enforced via OpenAI spend limits (acknowledged in code)
No authentication or moderation layer — any actor can post reports or mark zones as 'stable', potentially spreading misinformation during an active disaster
Single contributor — bus-factor risk for an app that may need rapid incident response
nodes.mergePotential: High — already deployed, clean architecture, public API, and PWA design make it the strongest mapping/coordination candidate in the field. The in-memory pagination scalability issue should be resolved before routing heavy traffic to it.

diffusion.angle: Frame around the real human problem: during Venezuela's M7.5 earthquake, help requests were drowning in social media noise. Apoyo Venezuela is the single live map that shows rescuers exactly where people are trapped, what each zone needs, and what is already covered — built by a Venezuelan developer in 4 days, no login required, works offline. Pair a screenshot of the map with a call to report your zone or share the link with anyone near the affected areas.
gaps: No demo video available — a 60-second screen recording of the map + reporting flow would dramatically increase shareability
No screenshots captured for social posts — the map with active zone markers would be a powerful visual
Zero stars/forks on GitHub — needs community amplification to reduce bus-factor risk and signal legitimacy
No social proof or real-user testimonials yet to cite in promotional content

nodes.name: Ayuda Venezuela
nodes.type: Telegram AI chatbot (Python) + Next.js landing page
nodes.summary: A Spanish-first Telegram bot that acts as an AI agent over the SOS Venezuela 2026 public humanitarian API, built for the Build4Venezuela 2026 hackathon. The project consists of two parts: a Python bot (the core product)…
nodes.onePitch: Venezuelans can find shelters, missing loved ones, and safe water just by asking @DEV_VEZbot on Telegram in plain Spanish — powered by live SOS Venezuela data, free, no app needed.

stack.orm: No database — all data comes from the public SOS Venezuela 2026 REST API via the team's surfcall/gecko-surf library
stack.pattern: Agentic tool-use loop (Claude Messages API) layered over a thin Telegram transport; landing page is a separate Next.js app

redFlags: No tests in this repo (bot.py hints tests may exist in the sibling surfcall repo, but they are not here)
No CI/CD pipeline
Hard dependency on gecko-surf (team's own library) — if it is not on PyPI or the repo disappears, the bot stops working
In-memory rate limiter resets on every process restart and does not work across multiple instances
No Dockerfile or deployment manifest — running in production requires manual setup
No logging infrastructure — failures in production would be invisible
Long-polling Telegram mode requires a persistent process, not suitable for serverless deployment
The 'canonical run path' described in README requires cloning a separate surfcall repo, adding deployment friction
nodes.mergePotential: The OpenAPI spec authored for SOS Venezuela 2026 is a standalone contribution that could be adopted by other projects. The Telegram bot is a complete, distinct channel that does not overlap with web-based projects. Could be integrated as a notification/query layer alongside mapping or shelter projects without conflicts.

diffusion.angle: Reach earthquake victims where they already are: just open Telegram and ask @DEV_VEZbot in Spanish where to find a shelter, whether the water is safe, or how to search for a missing person — no app download, no signup, no cost.
gaps: No demo video showing an actual Telegram conversation with the bot
No screenshots of the bot responding to real queries
No confirmed evidence of real users or usage data
The bot's live uptime is not independently verifiable without Telegram access
Dependency on gecko-surf (team's own library) is a fragility not explained in the landing page pitch

nodes.name: AyudaVE — Red P2P de Coordinación Humanitaria
nodes.type: Full-stack humanitarian web platform (Node.js monolith + vanilla-JS SPA)
nodes.summary: AyudaVE is a comprehensive humanitarian coordination platform built for the June 2026 La Guaira earthquake in Venezuela, already live at ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com. It is a full-stack Node.js monolith (server.js at 1…
nodes.onePitch: AyudaVE is the missing coordination layer for Venezuela's earthquake response — connecting donors, volunteers, and verified aid centers in real time, with AI-powered photo search for missing persons and zero mandatory sign-up.

stack.orm: Raw parameterized SQL via a thin adapter (store.js selects store-sqlite.js or store-pg.js). The adapter normalizes SQLite '?' placeholders to Postgres '$N' at query time. No migrations framework — schema is created via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS at init().
stack.pattern: Monolith — single server.js file handles routing, business logic, static file serving, and background data refresh loops

redFlags: Zero automated tests — no unit, integration, or e2e tests anywhere in the repo
No CI/CD pipeline (no .github/workflows or equivalent)
In-memory rate limiting (Map) won't work correctly with multiple Cloud Run replicas — a surge could bypass limits
Session table has no TTL or garbage collection — will grow unboundedly in production
CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' for script-src, weakening XSS protection
Only 5 git commits (though the codebase is substantial, indicating large initial commits rather than iterative development)
nodes.mergePotential: High — the platform is already live and operational, covering more humanitarian domains than almost any other project. It would be a strong anchor to merge other specialized tools into (e.g., a dedicated mental-health or comms-chat module). Its open REST API and documented data model make integration straightforward.

diffusion.angle: Lead with the AI photo-search for missing persons — it is the most emotionally resonant and technically impressive feature. Frame AyudaVE as 'the coordination layer the earthquake response was missing': one link, no registration required, connects donors with verified aid centers in under a minute. Secondary angle: contrast the chaos of WhatsApp groups with the structured P2P network AyudaVE provides, using the real data integrations (hospital patients, USGS seismic, structural damage) as proof of depth.
gaps: Live UI screenshot not confirmed (403 blocked crawler — team should provide screenshots or a screen recording walkthrough)
No real-user testimonials or adoption numbers yet (platform is 2 days old)
WhatsApp integration listed as future work — would massively increase reach in Venezuela

nodes.name: Beacon Rescate
nodes.type: Android mobile app (Python/Kivy, offline-first)
nodes.summary: Beacon Rescate is a single-file (~301 LOC) Python/Kivy Android mobile app intended to help rescue workers locate trapped victims in earthquake zones using offline BLE and Wi-Fi proximity scanning. The entire application…
nodes.onePitch: Beacon Rescate detecta el teléfono de víctimas atrapadas usando BLE y Wi-Fi sin internet — pero necesita corregir bugs críticos antes de ser útil en campo.

stack.orm: Raw sqlite3 calls with parameterized queries; WAL journal mode enabled; no ORM
stack.pattern: Single-file monolith

redFlags: Wi-Fi ping subprocess uses Windows-only flags (`ping -n 1 -w 100`) — silently fails on Android/Linux, breaking Wi-Fi mode entirely
Radar SQL query uses `LIMIT 1` so only the single most-recent device is ever displayed — the multi-victim visualization is non-functional
README claims Cython/C math integration (`calcular_distancia_rssi` in C) but all math is pure Python
procesar_dispositivo_wifi is defined in the App class but MonitorWifiHotspot calls its own guardar_en_bd_unificada directly, bypassing the app-level callback — a dead code path
No tests, no CI, no Buildozer spec file (.spec) — cannot actually build an APK from this repo as-is
Windows Zone.Identifier artifact (main.pyZone.Identifier) committed to repo — indicates development on Windows, raising doubts about Android testing
nodes.mergePotential: Low. The offline BLE proximity-scanning concept for rescue is distinctive and could complement web/server-based projects, but the code has functional bugs and would need significant rework before integration or deployment.

diffusion.angle: Offline BLE/Wi-Fi proximity scanning lets a single rescuer's phone detect trapped victims' devices with zero network infrastructure — the right idea for post-earthquake Venezuela.
gaps: demo-video
live-demo
screenshots
working-APK
branding
real-users

nodes.name: BuscaChat
nodes.type: WhatsApp / web chatbot for missing-persons search and family reunification
nodes.summary: BuscaChat is a production-deployed WhatsApp (and web) chatbot for family reunification after the Venezuela earthquake. It syncs missing-persons data from the sosvenezuela2026.com API into PostgreSQL+pgvector, exposes a…
nodes.onePitch: BuscaChat lets Venezuelan earthquake victims search for missing relatives via WhatsApp — by name, cedula, or photo — using facial recognition connected to the live sosvenezuela2026.com crisis database.

database: PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector (HNSW index for face embeddings)
ai_ml: InsightFace (buffalo_l ONNX model, face embedding + cosine similarity)
stack.orm: SQLModel (SQLAlchemy ORM) for models and queries; raw PostgreSQL dialect INSERT ON CONFLICT for upserts; Alembic for schema migrations (4 versions); pgvector extension managed via migration
stack.pattern: Layered FastAPI service with adapter pattern for multi-channel messaging and external data sources

redFlags: No rate limiting on public search and listing endpoints — could be abused or DoSed
Default private_api_token is 'dev-hackathon-token' in config.py (must be overridden in production .env, but easy to forget)
InsightFace runs on CPU (ctx_id=-1) — face embedding is slow without GPU; may bottleneck under load
APScheduler runs in-process: multi-instance deployment would double-sync the external API without coordination
In-memory conversation state is the default; a restarted server loses all active conversations
nodes.mergePotential: High — this project occupies a unique niche (WhatsApp chatbot for family reunification with facial recognition) that complements shelter-mapping or aid-logistics tools. The REST API (/missing_people) could be consumed by other projects in the hackathon. The adapter pattern makes adding Telegram or SMS channels straightforward.

diffusion.angle: Lead with the WhatsApp angle — 'busca a tu familiar perdido directo desde WhatsApp' — and the facial recognition capability. Emphasize that it connects to the real sosvenezuela2026.com database already being used in the crisis. Target Venezuelan diaspora communities (Peru, Colombia, Ecuador) who are trying to locate relatives. The multi-country coverage in the project metadata makes it directly relevant to the diaspora audience.
gaps: No working WhatsApp number or QR code exposed on the landing page — users cannot actually try the bot
No demo video showing the chatbot conversation flow end-to-end
No screenshots of actual WhatsApp interactions in the chatbot
Telegram links are placeholders — unclear if Telegram channel exists
Real-users signal is missing — no testimonials or usage metrics shown

nodes.name: Centraliza Ayuda
nodes.type: Static web directory / resource aggregator
nodes.summary: A pure static-site directory aggregating 130+ Venezuelan earthquake relief resources. Hosted on GitHub Pages with no backend. All data is embedded as a JS const array (data_source_json.js) maintained via a Python merge/…
nodes.onePitch: 130+ recursos de ayuda al terremoto Venezuela — personas desaparecidas, refugios, salud y donaciones, todo en un directorio con búsqueda y filtros, abierto a la comunidad.

stack.orm: None — data is a hardcoded JS const SOURCES array in data_source_json.js, built by merge_sources.py from a JSON flat file
stack.pattern: Static site with vanilla JS class-based MVC pattern

redFlags: API baseUrl hardcoded to http://localhost:3000 — the future backend is unbuilt
All fetch/API calls in api_manager.js are commented out behind a TODO comment
Content updates require a full git commit cycle — no live CMS or admin panel
No tests of any kind (unit or integration)
No CI/CD pipeline
Adding new resources requires developer intervention despite Google Form submission pathway
nodes.mergePotential: High — this is already a meta-aggregator that lists other hackathon projects (id 100, 103, 115). It could naturally absorb links to winning Build4Venezuela projects and serve as a persistent discovery layer. Its PR-based data pipeline is low-friction for integrators.

diffusion.angle: Position it as the central nervous system of Venezuela earthquake aid: '130+ recursos de ayuda al terremoto en Venezuela, en un solo lugar — búscalos por categoría o texto libre.' Emphasize the breadth (missing persons, refugios, salud, donaciones, mapas) and that it is open to community contributions. Call for organizations to submit their resource via the Google Form. Works best as a retweet/share anchor — give the URL and one screenshot of the directory in action.
gaps: no demo video showing the search and tag filtering in action
no screenshots committed to the repo or linked from README
no branding — site has no logo, icon, or distinctive visual identity
no confirmed real-user testimonials or usage metrics
README is minimal — no elevator pitch or impact statement that can be copy-pasted for promotion

nodes.name: Confia
nodes.type: Web app + REST API + Telegram bot (Next.js 16 / React 19, serverless on Vercel)
nodes.summary: Confía is a zero-onboarding misinformation and donation-scam verifier built for the June 2026 Venezuela earthquake. Users paste any forwarded WhatsApp message or crypto wallet address and receive a VERIFICADO / FALSO /…
nodes.onePitch: Pega cualquier rumor o dirección cripto en Confía y en segundos sabes si es verdad, falso o una estafa — sin instalar nada, sin registrarte.

stack.orm: No database — all knowledge is static TypeScript data files (rumors, resources, official channels, verified wallets). Live data comes exclusively from Solana JSON-RPC calls.
stack.pattern: Serverless, stateless Next.js App Router with a single shared verdict engine

redFlags: Verified-wallet allowlist contains only Confía's own donation addresses — no Cruz Roja, UNICEF, or other established NGO addresses are pre-verified, undermining the core trust proposition
In-memory rate limiter resets on every cold start; on a viral traffic spike across many serverless instances it provides no global protection against RPC quota exhaustion or LLM abuse
Zero tests — the forensics heuristics and pattern-matching logic have no automated coverage
No CI pipeline — a bad rumor entry or wrong wallet address could ship without any gate
next.config.ts is empty (default config) — no security headers, no CSP, no CORS restrictions
nodes.mergePotential: High standalone viability — already deployed, Spanish-first, no-install, directly combats donation fraud which other hackathon projects (donation rails) inadvertently amplify. Could be integrated as a verification widget or linked from any donation UI. The clean REST API (POST /api/verify) makes embedding straightforward.

diffusion.angle: Lead with the scam-wallet forensics angle: 'Before you donate, paste the address.' That is the most concrete, shareable, and instantly understandable use case for the diaspora audience. Secondary angle for inside Venezuela: 'Paste the rumor, get the truth in seconds — no app, no login.' Pair with a 30-second screen recording of the two demo flows (scam wallet → red flag, fake aftershock rumor → FALSO) and it becomes highly shareable on Twitter/X, WhatsApp channels, and Venezuelan diaspora Telegram groups.
gaps: No demo video — a 30-second screen recording of both key flows would dramatically increase shareability
No screenshots provided for social cards or press kits
Verified-wallet allowlist contains only the project's own addresses, not Cruz Roja or UNICEF — this needs to be disclosed or fixed before heavy promotion to avoid trust backlash
No real users / adoption signals yet (2 stars, 1 contributor, 7 commits)

nodes.name: VZLA_DEDUP
nodes.type: Data ingestion pipeline / ETL scraper for missing-persons and aid records
nodes.summary: VZLA_DEDUP is a Python data pipeline that scrapes, deduplicates, and sanitizes person/event/aid-center records from multiple Venezuelan earthquake relief sources, then exports them to a Supabase staging API. The scraper…
nodes.onePitch: VZLA_DEDUP unifies missing-persons and aid-center records from every fragmented source into one clean, deduplicated, PII-safe dataset — the data infrastructure backbone that every earthquake-relief app in Venezuela can build on.

stack.orm: Raw sqlite3 for local dedup fingerprint state; external Supabase backend accessed via httpx REST calls (no ORM)
stack.pattern: Linear pipeline with discrete stages: Adapter → Parser → PII tokenization → Enrichment → Confidence scoring → Minor protection → Staging export

redFlags: api/main.py, api/auth.py, and api/routes/records.py are all 1-line empty stubs — no serving layer exists
shared/config.py and shared/storage.py are empty — abstractions declared but not implemented
Only 1 of 5+ planned parsers (Encuentralos) is implemented and active; seismic sources (USGS, GDACS, ReliefWeb, Copernicus) are disabled with no registered parsers
SQLite dedup state (runtime_output/dedup_state.db) is ephemeral per GitHub Actions runner — parallel matrix jobs do not share dedup state, risking cross-run duplicates
No Dockerfile or deployment config — the system runs exclusively on GitHub Actions cron, which has monthly minutes limits and no persistent state between runs
verification/ package is an empty stub with only __init__.py
nodes.mergePotential: High — this project is explicitly designed as a data supply layer and would pair well with any people-finder or family-reunification front end. Its /api/aportes contract and deterministic external_id scheme are clean integration points. Any project needing deduplicated person/event/acopio records should consider consuming this pipeline's Supabase output rather than building their own scraper.

diffusion.angle: Technical infrastructure spotlight targeting other hackathon teams and developers: position VZLA_DEDUP as the clean-data backbone that any people-finder or rescue-logistics app should plug into. Emphasize the 15-volunteer team, 70 commits in 3 days, and the merge potential rather than a live product demo. Frame it as 'the data layer Venezuela was missing' and invite other teams to integrate against its Supabase staging endpoint.
gaps: No public API endpoint or web interface — consumers cannot access the data
No demo video showing the deduplication pipeline in action
Only 1 of 5+ planned parsers is implemented — data coverage is very narrow
No screenshots or visualizations of deduplicated output
SQLite dedup state is ephemeral per GH Actions runner, meaning parallel ingestion jobs can produce cross-run duplicates — a correctness risk worth disclosing

nodes.name: Directorio sismo
nodes.type: Static HTML resource directory / curated link aggregator
nodes.summary: Venezuela Solidaria (Directorio Sismo) is a manually curated static HTML directory that aggregates ~40+ external aid resources for Venezuela earthquake victims, organized into categories: health, emergencies, missing pe…
nodes.onePitch: Directorio Sismo centralizes 40+ verified aid resources for Venezuela earthquake victims — missing persons, shelters, health, and donations — in one bilingual directory updated in real time.

stack.orm: None — no database exists; all content is hardcoded HTML
stack.pattern: Static single-page site with no backend

redFlags: Admin password hardcoded in plaintext in public client-side JavaScript: 'Venezuela2025!' visible to anyone who views page source
No backend — all content is hardcoded HTML requiring manual edits to update
Single contributor, no CI, no automated deployment pipeline
Admin panel provides no real administrative function — it only shows instructions for drag-and-drop Netlify deployment
Content staleness risk: last-updated timestamp is hardcoded in HTML and must be manually changed
No analytics, monitoring, or uptime alerting
nodes.mergePotential: This project is a curated link directory that aggregates other projects — it is complementary to, not competitive with, functional apps. It could serve as an entry point or discovery layer that links to winning hackathon projects. Its main value-add for merging would be its curated external link database and bilingual UX, which could be embedded into a more capable platform.

diffusion.angle: Lead with the humanitarian urgency and the curation angle: 'Every link verified. One place. When seconds count.' Highlight the bilingual reach (EN/ES), the 8 categories covering every phase of earthquake response, and the fact that it is live and updated in real time today. Target both Venezuelan diaspora sharing with family and international donors looking for trusted entry points.
gaps: No screenshots or visual preview assets beyond the OG image
No real-user testimonials or usage metrics
No social proof (0 stars, 0 forks on GitHub)
Single contributor creates a bus-factor risk that should not be highlighted in promotional content

nodes.name: Doomedag
nodes.type: Full-stack web PWA + React Native mobile — emergency location-sharing and mesh communications platform
nodes.summary: iFoundYou (branded "Dommedag") is a real-time location-sharing, emergency-alerts, and offline-mesh-communications PWA with a React Native mobile companion. It uses Supabase for auth, database (PostGIS), and realtime sub…
nodes.onePitch: Dommedag lets earthquake survivors share GPS locations, broadcast emergency alerts, and chat over Meshtastic radios — even when the internet is down.

stack.orm: Supabase JS client (supabase-js) used directly — no ORM. Raw PostGIS SQL for spatial queries. IndexedDB via idb library for offline cache.
stack.pattern: BaaS + Serverless Functions + Offline-first PWA

redFlags: App.tsx is 2357 lines — all state management, effects, and UI in one god component, making contribution and maintenance fragile
No CI/CD pipeline — no automated build or test validation on push
Single trivial mobile snapshot test with no assertions — effectively zero test coverage
BridgeManager inserts lat:0/lon:0 into community_alerts for mesh messages without embedded location — creates corrupt geospatial data
No rate limiting on any Netlify Function endpoint — vulnerable to alert spam or location-flooding
Hardcoded production Netlify URL (imaginative-rolypoly-7fda4b.netlify.app) inside App.tsx shareApp function
WiFi scanner module (wifi/mac_spoofer.py) includes MAC address spoofing — potential legal/ethical concern in deployment contexts
8+ research markdown files in repo root (do deep reseach about Loopt app..., googlereseach2.md, etc.) are noise that indicate fast-and-loose development hygiene
App is branded Dommedag (Norwegian for Doomsday) with no localization to Spanish — requires i18n work before Venezuela deployment
nodes.mergePotential: Medium. The offline mesh bridge and geo-tagged community alerts are directly deployable for earthquake response and could integrate with shelter-mapping or aid-logistics tools. The PostGIS spatial layer and RLS-secured schema are reusable. Would require: splitting App.tsx, adding CI, configuring a Supabase project, and localizing the UI to Spanish.

diffusion.angle: If promoted at all, lead with the offline mesh networking angle — 'communicates without internet via Meshtastic radios' is a genuinely unique differentiator — and pair it with a short screen-recording showing the map and alert categories. Do not promote before Spanish localization and a working demo walkthrough exist.
gaps: No demo video whatsoever
No screenshots confirmed
Branding is 'Dommedag' (Norwegian for Doomsday) — must be localized to Spanish before Venezuela deployment
Live demo shows only a title in automated checks — no visible working flow for a promoter to reference
No license — contributors and NGOs cannot legally fork/deploy
UI language appears to be English, not Spanish — a barrier for Venezuelan users
MAC address spoofing module raises legal/ethical concerns that complicate public promotion

nodes.name: Crisis Map
nodes.type: Collaborative disaster-response incident mapping PWA (Angular frontend + AWS serverless backend)

nodes.mergePotential: High — solves a distinct and critical problem (post-earthquake building triage) not covered by other likely submissions. Offline-first design, standards-based methodology, and collaborative map make it immediately deployable. The AI crack analysis adds a meaningful augmentation layer. Main gaps (no tests, no CI, no rate limiting on AI endpoint) are fixable in hours.

diffusion.angle: Lead with the offline-first angle tied directly to the June 24 Venezuela earthquakes: 'When the cell towers went down, inspectors needed to keep working — Reestructuramos triages earthquake-damaged buildings with zero internet, GPS-stamped evidence, and ATC-20 placards that Protección Civil and FUNVISIS can trust.' The QR placard printout and the live damage map are highly visual assets perfect for social content. The demo video should anchor the post.
gaps: No real-user testimonials or adoption numbers yet (launched same day as hackathon)
Screenshots not explicitly catalogued — need screen captures of the wizard, the placard result, and the PDF output for social posts
Star/fork count is zero — no social proof signal yet

nodes.name: responsegrid.app
nodes.type: Full-stack monorepo web platform (Next.js frontend + NestJS REST API + PostgreSQL + Redis)
nodes.summary: ResponseGrid is a production-deployed (live at responsegrid.app + api.responsegrid.app) humanitarian material-aid coordination platform built for Venezuela's 2026 earthquake. It is a pnpm monorepo with a NestJS 11 hexag…
nodes.onePitch: ResponseGrid coordinates Venezuela earthquake relief in real time — matching verified donations to actual needs across 574+ collection points, live now at responsegrid.app.

stack.orm: Drizzle ORM with typed query builder; raw SQL strictly discouraged and documented as footgun; 36 hand-written versioned .sql migrations applied by a custom idempotent runner
stack.pattern: Hexagonal / DDD (ports & adapters) with CQRS-light and domain events

redFlags: Email notifications are not implemented (NoopSetPasswordInviter logs a TODO instead of sending emails — donor passwordless accounts and invitations are silently dropped)
No rate limiting on public map/resource endpoints (GET /emergencies/:id/resources, in-bounds queries) — high risk of unintentional DDoS during disaster peak
Internal package name `reliefhub` diverges from external brand `ResponseGrid` (minor inconsistency, not a blocker)
Single EC2 t3.small hosts API + PostgreSQL + Redis — no horizontal scaling plan documented beyond 'stateless so you can scale'
SetPasswordInviter Noop means any user invited to an organization as a service account never receives their credentials
nodes.mergePotential: High. The platform is already live, covers the core material-aid coordination use case comprehensively, has real Venezuelan data seeded, and is engineered for production. It could serve as a primary coordination tool immediately. The main integration work would be: (1) wiring a transactional email provider for the Noop inviter, and (2) ensuring the EC2 instance can handle disaster-scale traffic (add rate limiting on map endpoints or put a CDN in front of the public resource API).

diffusion.angle: Lead with the live map: 574+ verified Venezuelan collection points, one active disaster, real coordinators already using it. The angle is 'built in 3 days, operational today' — contrast the 274 commits, 168 tests, and Datadog monitoring against typical hackathon boilerplate to signal this is real infrastructure, not a demo. Show the verification badge system as the differentiator: donors and victims can trust what they see.
gaps: no demo video to embed in social posts
no screenshots available for static social cards
email notification flow not implemented — invited users silently receive no credentials, which limits onboarding at scale

nodes.name: ResQLink
nodes.type: Next.js 16 PWA — Humanitarian response coordination platform
nodes.summary: ResQLink AI is a humanitarian response PWA built with Next.js 16, Supabase (PostgreSQL + PostGIS + Realtime), and OpenAI GPT-4o-mini, targeted at earthquake emergency coordination in Venezuela. It implements Clean Archi…
nodes.onePitch: ResQLink AI conecta a víctimas de terremotos con voluntarios y recursos en tiempo real — reporta una emergencia con GPS desde el navegador y la IA la prioriza en segundos.

ai_ml: OpenAI GPT-4o-mini (classify priority, detect duplicates, recommend resources, summarize incidents)
stack.orm: Supabase JS client (BaaS) with raw SQL migrations. No ORM — queries are written directly via supabase-js fluent builder in src/infrastructure/supabase/queries/. PostGIS WKB hex output is decoded by a hand-written parseWKBPoint() function in src/shared/utils/geo.ts.
stack.pattern: Clean Architecture (Domain / Infrastructure / Presentation / App)

redFlags: AI API routes (/api/ai/classify, /api/ai/recommend, /api/ai/duplicates) have zero authentication or rate limiting — publicly callable, direct OpenAI cost exposure
No tests of any kind (unit, integration, e2e)
No CI/CD pipeline
env.ts uses TypeScript non-null assertions without runtime validation — silent crashes if env vars are missing in production
Death-toll extraction in sync-feed.mts uses regex over news text and writes results as authoritative incident records — this is fragile and could produce misleading official data
No pagination on incident/resource queries — table scans will degrade at scale
reporter_id hardcoded as 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000 for automated USGS incidents — breaks referential assumptions
Single contributor, 3-day-old repository, 15 commits — very limited battle-testing
nodes.mergePotential: Medium-High. The platform has a well-structured domain model (Incident, Resource, Hospital, Shelter, Volunteer, Organization) that could serve as a canonical data layer for aggregation. The Supabase Realtime infrastructure and PostGIS schema are reusable. The main integration concern is that all three AI routes are public endpoints — merging into a shared platform would require adding auth middleware to those routes first.

diffusion.angle: AI-powered real-time disaster coordination — anyone can report a GPS-tagged emergency from their phone, an AI instantly prioritizes it, and a live map shows victims, shelters, and volunteers updating in real time. Deployable today as a PWA with no app install required.
gaps: No real users or usage data yet
No screenshots of the map/dashboard in action for social posts
Single contributor limits perceived credibility for institutional adoption
Security issues (open AI endpoints) must be patched before promoting to high-traffic audiences

nodes.name: SARA Sistema de Apoyo y Respuesta Accesible para Emergencias
nodes.type: Emergency aid coordination web platform (full-stack: REST API + Next.js frontend)
nodes.summary: SARA (Sistema de Apoyo y Respuesta Accesible) is a hackathon project targeting aid coordination for people with disabilities after the Venezuela earthquake. It has a legitimately well-engineered Node.js/Express + Postgr…
nodes.onePitch: SARA conecta a personas con discapacidad con voluntarios y organizaciones cercanas mediante solicitudes geolocalizadas durante emergencias — porque la accesibilidad también salva vidas.

stack.orm: Raw parameterized SQL via node-postgres Pool. No ORM. Query construction is encapsulated in query.js and inline in route handlers.
stack.pattern: Layered REST API with module-per-operation on the backend; static-rendered Next.js App Router pages on the frontend (no data fetching)

redFlags: Frontend makes zero API calls — all pages render hardcoded mock data; the SOS form submits via formAction='/chat' (page navigation), not a fetch to the backend
Map page (/mapa) contains the literal comment 'Integra Google Maps o Mapbox para marcadores en tiempo real' — no map integration exists
CORS fully open with no origin restriction (app.use(cors()))
No authentication or rate limiting — any anonymous actor can accept or resolve any open help request
No Docker, no CI/CD, no deployment configuration of any kind
Registration form (/registro) has no onSubmit handler and no server action
Urgency values in the frontend form ('alta', 'media', 'baja') do not match the backend enum ('high', 'medium', 'low') — integration would produce validation errors
nodes.mergePotential: The backend module structure (routes/helpRequests, validation, query building, shared constants) is clean enough to be adopted directly. The geo-search CTE pattern and DB-level state machine constraints are worth copying. The frontend UI components (CategoryCard, PointCard, SOSButton, BottomNav, Navbar) are accessible and well-styled but need real data wiring before they can contribute value.

diffusion.angle: If promoting now, lead with the mission and the human stories (José losing his wheelchair, María needing a hearing aid replacement) rather than the product itself. Frame as 'a team building the emergency coordination layer that disability organizations have always needed — and they need your support to finish it.' Do not claim a working product exists.
gaps: No deployed demo URL — nothing a victim, donor, or journalist can click on
No demo video showing even the UI mockup in motion
No screenshots in the repo
Frontend-backend integration is entirely absent — the product is non-functional end-to-end
Urgency value mismatch between frontend ('alta'/'media'/'baja') and backend enum ('high'/'medium'/'low') would cause validation errors on first real use
No authentication, rate limiting, or abuse protection before any public launch

nodes.name: Sismología VE
nodes.type: Android app + Progressive Web App + Python scraper — seismic monitoring and real-time alert system for Venezuela
nodes.summary: SismologiaVE is a multi-component seismic monitoring system for Venezuela consisting of: (1) an Android app (Kotlin/Compose) that embeds a WebView loading the web UI and runs a foreground service polling USGS + EMSC + F…
nodes.onePitch: Sismología VE delivers real-time Venezuelan earthquake alerts combining three seismic data sources — including FUNVISIS, which has no public API — with an offline-capable Android app and PWA that works even when connectivity fails.

database: None — JSON files on IONOS shared hosting (sismos_venezuela.json)
stack.orm: No ORM or database. Data is stored as GeoJSON files on IONOS and SharedPreferences on device for seen-ID deduplication.
stack.pattern: Polling + Static JSON distribution — no server-side API, data flows: FUNVISIS → Python scraper (cron/1min) → IONOS JSON file → Android foreground service + web app fetch

redFlags: CRITICAL SECURITY: SSH credentials (host, user, password) committed in plaintext to DEPLOY.md — now public on GitHub
CRITICAL SECURITY: Android keystore password committed in plaintext in app/app/build.gradle.kts
PHP cron endpoint token exposed in public DEPLOY.md and URL
DataRepository.kt is a stub emitting hardcoded List<String>("Android") — the MVVM layer is unwired scaffolding
MainScreenTest uses FAKE_DATA and tests a function signature that does not match the actual MainScreen composable — tests would fail
Production APK intentionally signed with debug key — reduces trust and creates update signature conflicts
WebView uses deprecated allowFileAccessFromFileURLs and allowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs (security flags disabled)
IONOS shared hosting is a single point of failure with no redundancy or monitoring
No CI pipeline; deployment is fully manual via SSH
Source code lives in a directory named 'test/' creating significant confusion
nodes.mergePotential: Medium-high for the seismic data and alert domain. The multi-source aggregation (USGS + EMSC + FUNVISIS) with deduplication logic and the offline map tile strategy are genuinely novel contributions worth reusing. The Android foreground service notification implementation is production-quality. However, the exposed credentials must be rotated before any merge, and the IONOS dependency would need to be replaced with more robust infrastructure for a hackathon deployment serving earthquake victims.

diffusion.angle: Frame it as the earthquake early-warning system Venezuela's government never built: real-time alerts combining three seismic data sources, an offline-capable map, and an Android app that works even when connectivity is degraded — built by a solo Venezuelan developer in 48 hours.
gaps: Critical security issue (SSH credentials and keystore password committed publicly to GitHub) must be rotated before any spotlight — linking to the repo exposes live server access
Live demo was not showing earthquake data during review — needs verification that the IONOS scraper is running and the data feed is live
No demo video to show the notification system and simulation mode in action
No screenshots or visual assets for social posts
Debug-signed APK distributed to end users reduces trust for a safety-critical app

nodes.name: TerraVE
nodes.type: Web app (Next.js full-stack)
nodes.summary: TerraVE is a Next.js 14 (App Router) + Supabase web platform for post-earthquake territorial intelligence. It combines citizen crowdsourcing of lost infrastructure (schools, clinics, pharmacies, markets) with AI-powered…
nodes.onePitch: TerraVE es un mapa vivo post-terremoto que combina memoria ciudadana e IA para documentar qué infraestructura existía y cuánto daño estructural quedó — para que Venezuela nunca pierda el registro de lo que fue.

stack.orm: Supabase JS client (@supabase/supabase-js v2) — fluent query builder, no ORM; direct REST calls also used in some routes (satelital, poblacion, dashboard)
stack.pattern: Next.js App Router monolith with BaaS backend

redFlags: AI model (google/vit-base-patch16-224) is a generic ImageNet classifier, not fine-tuned for structural earthquake damage — classification results will be meaningless in real use
PATCH /api/report/[id] passes raw request body to supabase.update() with no field allowlist — mass-assignment vulnerability allows any authenticated-less caller to overwrite any column
No authentication on any write endpoint; Supabase RLS policies explicitly allow unrestricted public insert — database can be spammed with junk reports
No rate limiting on /api/classify (HuggingFace calls) or image upload — HF token can be exhausted by a single malicious user
satelital and poblacion API routes query tables (dano_satelital, poblacion_expuesta) that are NOT defined in the committed supabase-schema.sql — those features will 404 on a fresh deploy
Server Supabase client uses anon key (not service role key) so server-side code has the same permissions as an anonymous browser user — RLS bypass is impossible
No tests and no CI pipeline for a system meant to guide disaster relief decisions
nodes.mergePotential: The concept is unique and complementary — no other submitted project combines pre-disaster memory mapping with post-disaster AI damage classification on a single geo platform. Worth integrating the citizen reporting + damage mapping feature. The AI classification pipeline needs a fine-tuned model swap before it is trustworthy in production.

diffusion.angle: Lead with the citizen memory-mapping concept — 'Venezuela no puede perder la memoria de su infraestructura' — and the interactive Leaflet map. Avoid highlighting the AI damage classification in promotional content until the model is swapped for a fine-tuned earthquake damage classifier. Show the reporting form and map UI rather than the metrics (which are empty). Best positioned as 'la plataforma que preserva la memoria territorial de Venezuela antes y después de cada sismo.'
gaps: No real data in live demo — metrics show dashes, making the map feature invisible to a first visitor
No demo video showing the full flow (report a location, upload a photo, see the map update)
AI classification is a generic ImageNet model — promoting this feature would mislead users about reliability
No screenshots of the map with populated data points
Zero community traction (0 stars, 0 forks, 1 contributor)

nodes.name: Venemed
nodes.type: Web platform (Next.js full-stack)
nodes.summary: VeneMed is a time-windowed medical-aid platform built for Venezuelan health centers and donors. Health centers publish supply requests (solicitudes) with 12/24/48-hour windows; donors browse anonymously and share links…
nodes.onePitch: VeneMed conecta donantes con hospitales venezolanos en tiempo real: los centros publican exactamente lo que necesitan, con una ventana de horas — y cuando se cubre, la solicitud desaparece.

stack.orm: Drizzle ORM with postgres-js driver (bypasses Supabase RLS intentionally; authorization enforced in server code)
stack.pattern: Next.js App Router with route-group separation (public/center/admin), RSC-first, Server Actions for mutations

redFlags: No structured logging or error observability (no Sentry, Pino, or equivalent) — silent server action failures reach users only as generic Spanish error messages
Single contributor, 17 commits, repo created June 28 2026 — very new, bus-factor risk
Cron triggered via GitHub Actions every 5 min as a workaround for Vercel Hobby plan cap — adds an external dependency for expiry correctness; documented but fragile
Phase 3 slices 3-4 (request detail actions: Finalizar/Extender, and reception toggle) are incomplete — centers cannot yet close or extend active requests from the UI
Supabase RLS is explicitly bypassed in favor of application-level auth — requires discipline to maintain as the codebase grows
nodes.mergePotential: High — the codebase is deployed, well-architected, and directly addresses the earthquake relief context. The time-windowed expiry model and WhatsApp-first auth are specifically designed for Venezuelan conditions. Can be merged into a broader relief portal as the medical-supply coordination surface.

diffusion.angle: Show the time-window mechanic as the hero: 'Hospitals post exactly what they need and for how long — donors act before the window closes, nothing gets wasted.' Lead with the live URL and the real active requests visible on the homepage. Emphasize WhatsApp-first auth as a design decision built for Venezuelan connectivity, not a workaround.
gaps: No demo video — a 60-second screen recording of the center posting a request and a donor sharing it would dramatically improve shareability
No evidence of real health centers onboarded beyond seed/demo data — social proof from an actual hospital would be powerful
No screenshots prepared for social posts
No license on the repo, which limits open-source narrative

nodes.name: Venezuela Juntos (Missing Persons Facial Recognition)
nodes.type: Web app — AI-powered family reunification via face recognition
nodes.summary: Venezuela Juntos is a face-recognition family reunification web app built for disaster relief. Families upload photos of missing persons; rescuers upload photos of found people with contact info. The app uses InsightFac…
nodes.onePitch: Venezuela Juntos uses ArcFace AI to automatically match photos of missing persons with found persons — reuniting families separated by disaster in seconds, not days.

stack.orm: SQLAlchemy 2.x DeclarativeBase ORM over SQLite; three tables: missing_persons, found_persons, matches. No migrations (uses create_all on startup).
stack.pattern: Monolithic server-rendered MVC

redFlags: No authentication anywhere — all victim photos and rescuer contact details are publicly accessible to anyone who visits the site
0.40 cosine similarity threshold is relatively permissive; false positives in disaster contexts (wrong family match) can cause serious harm
O(N) full embedding scan on every upload — performance degrades linearly with database size
SQLite with check_same_thread=False is not safe for concurrent write load
No file size limit on photo uploads — potential for denial-of-service via large files
No deduplication — same photo uploaded twice creates a self-match
ThreadPoolExecutor limited to 2 workers — third concurrent ML request blocks
No logging — no way to audit who uploaded what or detect abuse
nodes.mergePotential: Unique in the hackathon for its AI face-matching approach to family reunification. No other project is likely doing ArcFace-based cross-matching. Strong candidate to merge or build upon if auth and privacy controls are added. The facerec.py library is reusable in other contexts.

diffusion.angle: Position as a technically impressive AI prototype for hackathon audiences and developers: 'ArcFace-powered family reunification — a real ML pipeline built in 24 hours to help find missing persons after Venezuelan disasters.' Emphasize the technology innovation and the humanitarian vision, NOT that victims should use it today. Reserve victim-facing promotion until auth and privacy controls are added.
gaps: No deployed public demo — victims cannot try it today
No authentication means all uploaded victim photos and rescuer contact details are publicly exposed (critical privacy blocker before any real promotion)
No screenshots in the repo for visual assets
0 stars/forks — no community signal yet
Single contributor, 8 commits — needs team expansion for trust

nodes.name: Venezuela Response Hub
nodes.type: Static informational hub / curated directory (SSR-capable SPA, deployed as static)
nodes.summary: A bilingual (ES/EN) static web hub that curates and links to ~20 community-built tools for the Venezuela earthquake response. Built with TanStack Start / React / Tailwind via the Lovable AI builder in under 48 hours. Al…
nodes.onePitch: Venezuela Response Hub: el directorio bilingüe que conecta a víctimas del terremoto y voluntarios globales con más de 22 herramientas verificadas — desaparecidos, albergues, ayuda médica y donaciones sin estafas, en un solo lugar.

ai_ml: Lovable (AI web builder used for generation)
stack.orm: None — no database of any kind. All data is hardcoded JS constants (SECTIONS array, EMERGENCY_NUMBERS, donation orgs, i18n dictionaries).
stack.pattern: Single-page application with optional SSR via TanStack Start/Nitro. Single route (index.tsx) contains all page logic.

redFlags: All data hardcoded — any content update (new tools, corrected phone numbers, changed drop-off locations) requires a code deploy by the original developer
Built entirely with Lovable AI builder; 306 commits in 2 days is AI iteration noise, not engineering depth
No tests, no CI, no containerization
Hardcoded emergency statistics ('1.430+ fallecidos', '68.900+ desaparecidos') will become stale with no mechanism to update
WhatsApp group link hardcoded in source — if group changes, requires deploy
The 'live' indicator in the header always shows 'En este segundo, hay personas siendo localizadas' — it is not actually live data
No mechanism for community tool submissions (contact email only, no form or CMS)
nodes.mergePotential: Low. This project is a static directory/hub that links OUT to other tools rather than providing standalone functionality. Its value is in curation and bilingual UX. It could be merged as a reference implementation for a navigation layer or landing page, but it duplicates the "aggregator" role that a main hackathon portal would already serve. The bilingual i18n implementation and trust-badge pattern could be borrowed.

diffusion.angle: Position as the bilingual front door to Venezuela earthquake relief: one URL that filters out scams, aggregates 22+ community tools, and tells you exactly where to go based on whether you're searching for a missing person, need shelter, want to donate, or are assessing damage. Highlight the trust-badge system and the fact that it went live within hours of the disaster.
gaps: No screenshots shared in submission materials for quick social use
No confirmed real-user testimonials or traffic numbers
The 'live' activity counter is fake — should not be highlighted in promo copy
Hardcoded statistics (deaths, missing) will become inaccurate over days, undercutting credibility if promoted widely after the acute phase
